posts.caption:
Few artists have probed the authorship, seduction and afterlife of circulated images as persistently as American conceptual artist Richard Prince. ⁠
⁠
At the @albertinamuseum in Vienna, a major exhibition brings together some 150 works, many never shown before. Focused on Prince’s photographic oeuvre from the 1970s to the present, it includes seminal series such as Fashion, Gangs and Cowboys, alongside sculpture, painting, collage and autobiographical imagery. Together, they broaden the view of Prince as a master of appropriation art in photography, dissecting the myths and media codes of American consumer culture.⁠
⁠
“Prince analysed how photography transforms our perception of the world,” Dr Walter Moser, chief curator of photography at Albertina Museum tells @sayori_radda. Photographs do not simply reflect reality, but “shape identity, desire and the way we see the world.” In a culture of copied and circulated images, shaped by misinformation and visual manipulation, Prince’s question cuts sharply: “If the copy is the original, what does that make the original?”⁠
⁠
Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
At Monday’s Met Gala, instead of arriving in another major fashion house look, Kim Kardashian appeared in an orange fibreglass breastplate referencing Allen Jones’s 2013 artwork Body Armour.
The orange fibreglass breastplate was based on an original Allen Jones mould from 1969, finished in a Kent car body shop with layers of automotive paint and gloss lacquer, while Whitaker Malem added the hand-painted leather skirt and Nadia Lee Cohen photographed the project.
The artists collaboration behind it feels almost too perfect: Allen Jones (@allenjonestheartist), Whitaker Malem (@whitakermalem), and Nadia Lee Cohen (@nadialeecohen) all working within the same visual language of glamour, fetish, sculpture, and the body as image.
In addition, Kim was such a perfect choice for this look. According to the duo Whitaker Malem, Jones’s breastplates were never really intended to be worn, yet Kim’s torso and silhouette fit them “remarkably” naturally. They even said they had never seen one fit anyone so well before.
How do you like this look?
Words by Josephine Jiang

posts.caption:
Now THIS is a music video.😍
Lady Gaga x Doechii just dropped “Runway” for The Devil Wears Prada soundtrack and the fashion delivered!
From black-and-white, spiked, bejeweled latex by Gaurav Gupta to Doechii in Harris Reed, the video layers sculptural couture with theatrical, archival-coded references.
Across the visual, looks pull from designers like Robert Wun and Luar, while referencing historical silhouettes Marie Antoinette-era dressing, exaggerated court shapes, and Gaga’s own ‘Telephone’ era fashion archive.
Every frame feels built on fashion history, reworked through a modern, performance-driven lens.

Movado (@movado) celebrates 145 years of Swiss craftsmanship with the launch of its Heritage 1917 collection in Mumbai. Inspired by the brand’s first square Art Deco watch from 1917, the collection revives a defining archival silhouette with contemporary refinement, featuring sunburst ombré dials, elegant leather straps, and Swiss automatic movement. Blending heritage design with modern engineering, the collection stands as a tribute to Movado’s enduring legacy of innovation, artistry, and timeless watchmaking excellence.

Summer reading calls for books that are as immersive as they are escapist. One longs to go to places elsewhere, be it different cities or different years. A courtyard humming with family gossip in Aligarh, the salt-heavy air along the Amalfi Coast, or jazz-filled Parisian nights after the war: these stories build entire worlds you can briefly inhabit. Consider this your literary itinerary for the season: emotionally rich, geographically indulgent, and best consumed on an outdoor picnic.

Resale has never been more mainstream. The global secondhand market is growing at twice the rate of firsthand retail, and is expected to reach $393 billion by 2030, according to Thredup’s 2026 resale report.
As the competition grows, resale platforms like Ebay, Poshmark, Thredup and Depop are upping their marketing game in order to tap into the momentum.
BoF's Haley Crawford explores how these platforms are leveraging everything from offline activations to live shopping experiences to stand out. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @depop, @ebay

posts.caption:
While sustainable practices are only increasing in importance with climate change, sustainability's shine as a selling point has fizzled out as shoppers are less moved by hearing about a brand’s carbon emissions or recycling efforts. What they are showing interest in, however, is how a product could affect their health.
Sustainable brands are rethinking how they communicate value. While continuing the work in the background, they're reframing their messaging around wellness, health and personal benefit, foregrounding attributes consumers can immediately understand, like breathability, softness or products free of chemicals.

posts.caption:
Since the earliest days of tools like ChatGPT and Claude, industry conversations have been marked by a tension between excitement around speed and efficiency alongside deep-seated fears of job loss, creative dilution and concerns about its environmental footprint. What once played out in theory is now unfolding in practice — as a broader rejection of what AI represents — particularly as more consumers view AI-generated content as a cost-cutting measure that erodes fashion’s human touch,
In this episode, The Debrief host Sheena Butler-Young (@sheena.butler.young) discusses with BoF correspondents Marc Bain (@marcbain_) and Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) why the backlash is intensifying and how consumer sentiment against brands using AI-generated imagery is forcing a reckoning. They explore whether fashion can actually embrace these tools without losing the care and time that confers luxury status.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast

posts.caption:
Writer, academic and activist Sinéad Burke (@thesineadburke) has donated two pieces from her private wardrobe to the permanent collection of the Met Museum’s Costume Institute, including a custom-made Burberry trench coat, scissored and shot by Tim Walker (@timwalker) for the cover of BoF’s ‘The Age of Influence’ special print issue in 2018.
The trench appears in the department’s latest exhibition, ‘Costume Art,’ which was unveiled with last night’s Met Gala and is, in part, organised around a series of thematic body types, including ‘the disabled body.’ Burke’s own body was scanned to develop the mannequin on which the coat is displayed.
“This is an explicit and radical invitation for disabled people to see themselves in fashion, to see themselves in the Met, to see themselves at the Costume Institute,” said Burke, who consulted on the exhibition.
Watch how the first mannequin of Burke’s body was created on The BoF Show’s third episode “Everybody is Different. Why Does Fashion Always Look the Same?” #linkinbio

Tonkin plays Emily, a woman with a complicated love and family life who sets out on a series of dates with Ryan, played by Brenton Thwaites, to work out if they’re destined to marry. “There was something quite relatable and ordinary about these two people who are in their 30s and trying to navigate dating, coming out of a pandemic, all the isolation and loneliness that they’ve been dealing with. And I just thought it was really real,” she shares. It was this funny, quirky love story drama that you don’t get to do often.”

posts.caption:
Generally speaking, you can't sit on the art in a museum. But in one gallery of the Museum of Contemporary Art Chicago (@mcachicago) — which is currently staged to resemble a karaoke bar complete with a disco ball, stage and jukebox — three plastic chairs, upholstered with the face of Puerto Rican superstar @badbunnypr, are waiting for you to rest between songs.
@edrasoto's "BB Chairs" — outfitted in bootleg fabrics featuring the singer with sunglasses and buzz cuts — have been a tongue-in-cheek nod to both the plastic white chair ubiquitous to the island and the performer's deep connection to his home.
"I had this idea a whole year before I made them," she said. "I was doubting myself. I was thinking maybe this is too on the nose."
But friends excitedly reached out to Soto when Bad Bunny released the now history-making, @grammys Award-winning album "DeBÍ TiRAR MáS FOToS." The album cover featured two empty white lawn chairs — an evocative symbol of home and belonging in Puerto Rico — and they had significance to Soto's practice, too.
"The furniture that I grew up with was wicker and plastic," she explained. "I asked myself what my chair would look like if I was making a chair." She said she couldn't relate to high-end materials, and began thinking about the fantasy of luxury in both the practice of upholstery and the colorful, if culturally inaccurate, images associated with the tropics.
📸 : Courtesy the artist/E G Schempf/Courtesy Kemper Museum of Contemporary Art
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on May 07, 2026 tagging @mcachicago, and @edrasoto. May be pop art of armchair, rocking chair, poster, chair and text that says 'THE BAD BUNNY CHAIRS TAKING OVER THE ART WORLD CNN'.

posts.caption:
The return of @bts.bighitofficial in March, nearly four years after they went on hiatus for mandatory military service, has highlighted a key question for the K-pop industry: What comes next?
This current generation has arguably grander ambitions – some of which beg the question: Does K-pop need to be Korean at all?
For instance, @blackpinkofficial's recently released mini-album "Deadline" is almost entirely in English. And new groups are debuting in other parts of the world, some with members that have no ties to South Korea.
"There's more global collaboration, and work within the K-pop world to become globalized, within every generation," said Danny Chung, the songwriter behind several major Blackpink hits.
Some of this is happening in other musical genres, too, with social media increasingly shaping how we consume and produce music. But it's especially true of K-pop, which is "ever-changing," said Chung, who also works in the Artists and Repertoire (A&R) team for record company The Black Label.
Experts say this fluidity could fuel the industry's aim to expand beyond borders and neat categorization.
"It's a forever moving target to try to define the genre of K-pop," Chung said.
📸 : Kim Hong-Ji/Pool/Getty Images/Christopher Polk/Billboard/Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/AFP

posts.caption:
The Steve Madden ads in the 2000s had a really distinctive style that made them instantly recognizable. Most of the campaigns featured models with oversized heads and tiny bodies, creating an almost doll-like or cartoon-inspired look. The editing was playful and exaggerated, which matched the bold energy of early 2000s fashion perfectly.
The ads usually placed the models in everyday settings like city streets, bedrooms, rooftops, or suburban neighborhoods, but everything felt slightly surreal and stylized. Bright colors, dramatic shoes, heavy makeup, and exaggerated poses gave the campaigns a fun and edgy vibe without taking themselves too seriously.
A big focus was always on the shoes. Whether it was chunky platforms, heels, boots, or sandals, the footwear stood out as the center of the image. The playful proportions and unusual compositions made the products feel trendy, youthful, and full of personality.
Looking back, the campaigns capture the spirit of Y2K fashion really well — experimental, confident, slightly chaotic, and very visual. Even today, the ads still feel iconic because they had such a unique identity compared to other fashion campaigns of the time.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 08, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster and text that says 'From the Archives: Steve Madden Ads in the 00s CULT®'.

posts.caption:
“Nobody does it like her.”
Nobody makes dark femininity feel as instinctive as Mariacarla Boscono. The sharp silhouettes, the restrained glamour, the balance between softness and severity — it all feels natural on her. In Of Love and Other Demons for d la Repubblica, photographed by Carlijn Jacobs, Mariacarla moves through sculptural looks and cinematic beauty with the kind of presence that can’t be taught.
That’s what has made her so influential in fashion for decades. Since the late 1990s, Mariacarla has become one of the industry’s most recognizable and respected faces, working with designers including Riccardo Tisci, Karl Lagerfeld, Prada, Givenchy, Dior, Valentino, McQueen, and Chanel. More than just a model, she represents a certain kind of fashion image: intelligent, emotional, slightly undone, and never overly polished.
Long before dark feminine aesthetics became mainstream again, Mariacarla was already defining them. Her ability to bring character and emotion into an editorial is what continues to make her relevant across generations of fashion.
Because nobody does it like her.
—

Photography: Carlijn Jacobs
Styling: Jacob K

Makeup: Lucy Bridge

posts.caption:
The Misshapes sit in a very specific corner of mid-2000s New York nightlife: post-electroclash, pre-Instagram, when a party could still feel like a private scene even while being photographed every week. With Geordon Nicol, Leigh Lezark, and Greg Krelenstein at the center, The Misshapes became less about one venue or one night and more about a certain downtown visual code.
The look was narrow, dark, and intentionally unfinished. Think black skinny jeans, shrunken leather jackets, smudged eyeliner, slept-in hair, etc. — but not in a polished “styled for a theme party” way. It was not Y2K in the glossy pop-star sense. It was colder, cheaper-looking, more nocturnal; like someone had crossed a fashion closet with a basement bar.
The dress code worked because it never looked fully styled. A designer piece could sit next to something thrifted, and the whole outfit had the feeling of being caught between a gallery opening, a cigarette outside, and a 3 a.m. flash photo. The attitude was just as important as the clothes: bored, angular, slightly inaccessible.
That is what makes the Misshapes reference still interesting now. It is not just “indie sleaze” as a trend, but a record of a moment when nightlife, fashion, photography, and social hierarchy all collapsed into one image. The clothes were simple, but the context made them feel coded. You were not just dressing for a party — you were dressing for the possibility of being documented.
In that sense, the Misshapes dress code was never really about looking perfect. It was about looking like you belonged to a scene that was already halfway mythologizing itself.
Important photo credits / references
* The Misshapes book photography contributors: Julian Gilbert, Karl Lagerfeld, Leigh Lezark, Nick Haymes, Miguel Villalobos, Patrick McMullan, Ryan McGinley, Ryan Pfluger, Scott Meriam, and Nick Zinner.

posts.caption:
This editorial presents Anja Rubik and Ibrahim Kamara in a poetic, gender-fluid visual dialogue for the May 2020 collaboration between Vogue Italia and L’Uomo Vogue. Photographed by Harley Weir, the story is built around the idea of mirroring: masculine and feminine codes reflect, overlap, and dissolve into one another. Vogue Italia describes the project as two complementary fashion stories across the women’s and men’s magazines, exploring gender in fashion and society through “a game of mirrors.”
The images feel intimate, theatrical, and quietly radical. Rubik and Kamara appear as both individuals and alter egos, moving through sharply styled tailoring, sculptural silhouettes, exposed skin, headpieces, and graphic beauty details. Kamara’s styling uses fashion as a language of transformation, placing classic menswear, couture-like gestures, and body-conscious styling into a space where identity becomes fluid rather than fixed. The result is less a conventional fashion editorial than a visual conversation about self-recognition, performance, and becoming.
Harley Weir’s photography gives the story a dreamlike tension: soft yet direct, sensual yet cerebral. The editorial’s strength lies in its balance between vulnerability and control. Rubik’s presence is fierce and statuesque, while Kamara brings a personal, performative energy that makes the story feel like an exchange between artist, stylist, model, and subject. Together, they create an editorial that treats gender not as a binary, but as a living, shifting surface—something seen, styled, reflected, and reimagined.
Credits: Photography: Harley Weir. Styling/Fashion Editor: Ibrahim “Ib” Kamara. Models: Anja Rubik and Ibrahim Kamara. Make-up: Hiromi Ueda, using Le Volume Stretch de Chanel and Boy de Chanel Le Teint. Hair: Cyndia Harvey, using Oribe. Manicure: Sylvie MacMillan. Set Design: Alice Kirkpatrick. Bondage Artists: Anna Barros and Erlend Davidson at Anatomie Studio. On Set: Shiny Projects. Stylist Assistant: Sasha Harris. Casting Director: Piergiorgio Del Moro. Editors: Emanuele Farneti and Thomas Persson. Publications: Vogue Italia and L’Uomo Vogue, May 2020.

posts.caption:
Born in Jamaica and raised between strict religion, rebellion, and reinvention, she became one of those rare figures who made Fashion feel bigger than just clothing. In the 1970s, she moved through New York and Paris as a model, but she never fit the soft, pretty mold the industry expected from women at that time. That was the point. Her beauty was sharp, sculptural, and impossible to soften.
Grace turned her body into architecture. The flat-top hair, the power shoulders, the dark sunglasses, the sharp tailoring, the metallic makeup. She played with masculinity and femininity before fashion had comfortable language for it. One moment she looked like a goddess, the next like a nightclub villain, the next like a living sculpture.
What made her different was that she didn’t wear fashion to look desirable. She wore it to look powerful. Designers, photographers, and artists didn’t just dress her, they built worlds around her. With Jean-Paul Goude, her image became surreal, graphic, almost superhuman. With designers like Azzedine Alaïa, her strength, shape, and confidence became part of the clothes themselves.
Her life moved between music, modeling, film, art, and performance, but fashion was always the thread connecting everything. She made disco feel dangerous, androgyny feel luxurious, and glamour feel untouchable.
Grace Jones didn’t become a fashion icon because she followed style.
She became one because she made style obey her.

posts.caption:
Spring 2026 could be looking icy in the best way.
It’s that cold, clean mix of black leather, sheer tights, silver details, slick hair and sharp little glasses. Everything feels a bit polished, a bit extra, but still cool.
The colors stay simple: black, white, grey, silver and icy blue. But the textures make it interesting. Glossy leather, soft fur, shiny bags, thin tights, metal details. It’s not too sweet, not too pretty, not trying too hard.
It feels like office siren’s colder sister, a little more mysterious, a little more dressed-up.
Less spring flowers, more frozen city girl.
*This Post is meant to serve as inspiration*

posts.caption: I don’t like the narrative that Brat wasn’t for the fans - I absolutely was. It was her most intimate, yet ridiculously good produced project - and everything that followed after the release was purely because certain parts of the era blew up and she took the opportunity to turn it into a cultural fenomenon. She’s never recorded an album with an intention to sell out. People may never react to her post-Brat music the same, but I’m pretty sure she’s aware of that and she’ll never try to make a song or an album to make people like her - you can like this or not, as her longtime fan I believe she’ll accept both options and won’t cry for streams.

posts.caption:
Tennessee Republicans just passed a new congressional map that redraws Memphis’ majority-Black Democratic district, a move critics say is designed to help the GOP keep control of the House.
Republicans claim the redistricting reflects population shifts, but opponents are calling BS, arguing the map is designed to dilute Black voting power.  “You cannot take a majority Black city, fracture its voting power and then tell us race has nothing to do with it,” Sen. London Lamar said.
The vote sparked major protests inside the state Capitol, with demonstrators filling the galleries alongside Democratic lawmakers. State Rep. Justin Jones burned a Confederate flag during the legislative session, while State Rep. Justin J. Pearson confronted THP troopers as they cleared protesters out. State Sen. Charlane Oliver stood on her desk holding a “No Jim Crow” banner before Republicans abruptly ended the session. Gov. Bill Lee signed the map into law shortly after.
The new districts come just days after SCOTUS weakened key Voting Rights Act protections, making Tennessee the first state to redraw congressional maps under the new ruling.

The fatal outbreak of hantavirus aboard a cruise ship sailing the Atlantic reportedly may have been contracted during a Dutch couple’s bird-watching trip at a landfill aka people playing in literal trash 🙃
Hantavirus is a rare, deadly disease primarily carried by infected rodents. So far, three passengers on the Oceanwide Expeditions cruise ship have died, with at least four others showing symptoms, according to the World Health Organization (WHO).
A KLM flight attendant who was in contact with a now deceased passenger is also showing symptoms and is being tested for hantavirus, authorities in the Netherlands have confirmed.
On Thursday, Oceanwide issued an update saying 30 guests disembarked the ship on April 24, nearly two weeks after the first passenger died on April 11. According to Oceanwide, the ship was carrying 88 passengers and 61 crew members.
Despite the outbreak, WHO insists there’s no need to panic, saying “the risk to the global population from this event as low.”
The US completed its withdrawal from the WHO in Jan 2026, following an Executive Order signed by President Trump on his first day in office last year. Oh, he also gutted the CDC, but no need to worry since RFK is at the helm now ::screams into pillow::

posts.caption:
This year’s Met Gala didn’t feel as fun as it used to. That’s in no small part due to the involvement of the Bezos’s, for whom the event has been dubbed the “Tech Gala.” But is the Tech Gala just a symptom of a disease that’s been festering for a long time now?
When the Met Gala began in 1948, it wasn’t the press-addled, celeb-fueled circus it is today, but was an elite dinner that benefitted the Met’s Costume Institute. In 1973, Diana Vreeland, Editor-in-Chief of Vogue, became involved and the event received its first splashy theme in honor of Balenciaga who had died the year previous. One year later, Cher wore the nude dress to the Gala which drew an unprecedented heap of press.
Cut to the Anna Wintour era when the event began to include stars from all walks of fame, and then to the 2010s when it became a mainstream pop culture moment. In 2015 the Gala not only came with its own documentary, but Rihanna wore that yellow Guo Pei gown with a 16’ train. The dress caused a media frenzy, transforming the way celebs went ‘all out’ for the event. And, in turn, it transformed the way the public engaged online: Social media commentary became commonplace, if not ubiquitous, forcing the event to inflate into the spectacle it is today.
But, because the Gala became the de facto measure of cultural clout, tech bro culture vultures bought their way onto the guest list this year. Their presence, though, was so unpopular that they couldn’t even show face on the carpet and an inordinate amount of protests cropped up. The public perception of the event took a loathsome turn, with many online comparing the event to The Hunger Games. Even some of the Gala’s favorite celebs dropped out for suspicious reasons.
Is there a future for this once glorious night?
Speaking about the Gala, Andrew Bolton, head of the Costume Institute, admitted “sometimes it dwarfs everything.” Thus, the Institute has saved up their proceeds and are working towards self-sufficiency. That means that the event may not be the philanthropic gesture that it once was, making it instead just a blown up photo-op.
But, where does that leave us? The spectators en masse? Have we grown out of it too?

COSTUME ART Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
Amidst a sea of lavish gowns at the Met Gala, nearly all of which were custom pieces crafted by the most prolific fashion houses on the planet, one look stood out for the exact opposite reason; Bhavitha Mandava appeared to be wearing a casual quarter-zip with straight cut blue jeans.
To be fair, there’s more to the look than meets the eye. First of all, the outfit is Chanel and it is, technically, an illusion made from silk, not denim. Second, it calls back to the outfit Mandava, a grad student at NYU, was wearing on the day she entered the Atlantic Avenue subway station and was ‘discovered’ by a modeling agent. She became a bright star in the modeling world at a meteoric pace, and in March of this year became the first Indian woman to hold a coveted Chanel ambassadorship.
But, the Met Gala look isn’t the first time Chanel has reinterpreted Mandava’s fated subway station look; she opened Chanel’s Pre-Fall ‘26 show, which was held on a defunct NYC subway platform, in a recreation of the same outfit. Why, then, was it necessary to churn the same look out at such an important event as the Met Gala?
Some fans, especially those with Indian and South Asian heritage, are enraged, calling the outfit “racist” and “a microaggression” and pointing out that the outfit would not have been given to a white model or ambassador. IG fashion commentator Sufi Motiwala said he was “pissed” and accused Chanel of “tokenizing” Mandava.
“Be serious,” said Anisha Ramakrishna Tarpara on IG, “the outfit to get on the subway, the outfit I wear to go to CVS. Chanel really said ‘we see India and we want to meet you underground.’”
The overly casual look which the design house has prioritized for their first ever Indian ambassador seems to peddle a narrative which excludes her from the glamour of her counterparts. Mandava’s Chanel counterparts, by the way, include Jennie, Margot Robbie, Lily Rose-Depp, Gracie Abrams, and Anna Wintour herself who each wore luxurious evening gowns to the Gala. As did Awar Odhiang, a fellow Chanel model of comparable viral fame. In a bathroom selfie taken by Mandava, the discrepancy of her jeans-and-a-casual-top outfit is impossible to ignore.
Thoughts?

At just 24 years old, the 10-time Grammy winner and two-time Oscar winner has existed in the public consciousness for a decade now. But this May, a new concert documentary attempts to capture her star power and stage presence in a new light—through the eyes of legendary director James Cameron, no less. Eilish never thought she’d make another documentary after 2021’s ‘Billie Eilish: The World’s a Little Blurry,’ which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. “I’m so private about my actual life now,” Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‘Avatar’ filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up—and co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
In the ’90s, @jewel rocketed to music superstardom with her 1995 debut ‘Pieces of You’ and 1998 follow-up ‘Spirit’—albums that helped define a particular strain of folk-pop with her introspection and disarming directness. Her songs read like diary entries, confessions that are once deeply personal and universally relatable. Now, this emotion also defines her work as a visual artist.
Now 51, the Grammy nominee is about to debut her first solo art show, “Matriclysm: An Archaeology of Connections Lost.” Held at the Salone Verde from May 7 to Nov. 22, 2026, the expansive exhibition will coincide with the 61st Venice Biennale. Thirty works, spanning painting, sculpture, tapestry, and sound installations, are arranged across a series of immersive rooms that unfold one into the next. The show moves through what she calls “matriclysm,” where themes of feminine inheritance, rupture, and repair sit side by side.
“I make work because I need it. I make work because something’s on my mind or in my heart, and I need to understand and explore it,” she says. “I’ve switched genres, I’ve switched mediums....You have to do what your heart tells you.”
Ahead of her big debut, Jewel opens up to ELLE about diving into a new industry—all while juggling motherhood and her music career on her own terms. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

At 39 years old, @emilywesterfield has spent the last decade of her life either pregnant or working to get pregnant.
In February, she gave birth to her 12th baby; she has also previously birthed two sets of twins. But Westerfield’s no trad wife, she’s a gestational carrier, better known as a surrogate. Of the 12 babies she’s carried, nine have been for other families. “It’s about helping other people attain their dream of becoming parents,” Westerfield says.
At the link in bio, Westerfield speaks to ELLE about her journey to surrogacy, how it led her to start her own agency, and the power in helping others become parents.

posts.caption:
If hopecore is what you're after, look no further. Isabel Klee (@isabelklee)—and her dog, Simon (@simonsits)—are here to save your algorithm.
To her audience of over 2 million followers on TikTok and Instagram, Klee offers daily updates as she fosters a series of hard-case medical and behavioral rescue dogs, then eventually sends them along to elated new owners.
Now, Klee’s first book—a sweet memoir of her twenties in New York called ‘Dogs, Boys, and Other Things I’ve Cried About’—is a number one New York Times bestseller. In nonlinear chapters alternating between the dogs and the men she’s loved, Klee tells of dating an addict, then a man who just didn’t fit, only to self-sabotage when she met her match, now-fiancé Jacob. During this time, she also met the great love of her life, Simon, a rescue from Korea whose canine idiopathic epilepsy motivated her to help other medically complex fosters.
In conversation with ELLE, Klee unpacks how dogs help make our timelines a little brighter, what they’ve taught her about love and resilience, and teases the upcoming TV adaptation of her debut memoir. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

posts.caption:
Fashion is built on its workers 💚⁠
⁠
As @zohrankmamdani shared with @i_d, the “fashion industry is made possible by the thousands of workers,” speaking to the supply chains that go unknown and unseen that keep the industry moving forward. All too often, these workers are underpaid and denied access to autonomy, largely due to brands rewarding those at the top at the expense of those at the bottom.⁠
⁠
When we think of sustainability, we often think of the planet and environments and animals. But, if the business isn’t sustainable for the people who make our clothes, then it’s not sustainable at all.⁠
⁠
Swipe for some ideas of how to help workers, then visit the link to learn more about this issue. How are you advocating for garment workers? Share in the comments, and tag a friend as a reminder of the power of people in fashion 🪡

Grace Victory has made a career of sharing her life online, but when it came to her abortion, she couldn’t bring herself to speak publicly.
‘As a mum and plus-sized mixed race woman, I already receive judgment from the public,’ she writes for Grazia. ‘I knew I wasn’t ready to face negative comments, so decided to only tell the people that I trusted most. posts.caption:
@chloessevigny is the real deal. For three decades, Sevigny has cut a singular path, both on screen and in fashion, building a body of work that feels as unexpected as it does authentically her own. In Bazaar’s May 2026 issue, the actor talks about her next move—“The Five-Star Weekend,” a drama series adapted from the Elin Hilderbrand novel in which she stars alongside Jennifer Garner. It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that’s precisely why it also makes sense; she’ll never fit neatly into any box. “My agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‘This is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,’” Sevigny says, “but I really feel there’s really something for everybody.”

posts.caption:
@ralphlauren built an entire universe.
Now, nearly 60 years after launching Polo ties in 1967, Thames & Hudson is celebrating his legacy with Ralph Lauren: Catwalk, the first American-focused edition in its iconic Catwalk series.
Packed with more than 1,300 runway images and commentary from fashion journalist Bridget Foley, the book traces Ralph Lauren’s evolution from his first womenswear collection in 1972 through Fall 2025. It highlights the signatures that made the brand timeless: sharp tailoring, effortless prep, Western influences, and Lauren’s knack for blending masculine and feminine style.
The book captures the consistency of his vision, proving why Ralph Lauren remains one of fashion’s most enduring creative forces.

posts.caption:
In Edo-period Japan, people didn’t throw fabric away. They repaired it, patched it, stitched it back to life. ⁠
⁠
Using a technique called Sashiko, farmers and working families turned worn clothes into layered pieces that could survive for generations. A single Boro coat might pass through four or five family members, each patch carrying a different season, a different story. What started as pure necessity became accidental art. Today, those once-humble “rags” sell for thousands in auction houses across Tokyo, Paris, and London.⁠
⁠
How many generations is your Kapital Boro jacket surviving?

⁠
Starting today, the 2027 Ford Super Duty Carhartt is available in the US through Ford dealers.⁠

posts.caption:
The quietest looks are always the loudest statements, and Avneet Kaur has known this for a while now. 🌸
Draped in the deep, unhurried cool of monochromatic indigo, she doesn’t chase the moment. She sets the pace for it. Denim-on-denim, lace against leather, coastal light and shadow, every element dialled into a frequency that’s entirely her own.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this isn’t just a fashion story. It’s a portrait of a generation that leads with composure, one that finds its power not in the loudest room, but in the stillest one. ✨
The industry moves fast. Avneet doesn’t rush. And somehow, she’s always exactly where the moment needs her to be.
Cool, collected, and completely in control, this is the indigo hour. And it belongs to her.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Il #Foulard di seta non è mai stato un accessorio. È un modo di stare al mondo. Jackie Kennedy-Onassis a Cozumel nel 1968 — foulard di seta annodato in testa, occhiali enormi, nient’altro. Audrey Hepburn sul set di Funny Face nel 1956, Sophia Loren sull’Acropoli nello stesso anno, Grace Kelly come firma personale, Queen Elizabeth II al Royal Windsor Horse Show. Quello che accomuna tutte queste immagini non è il pezzo in sé. È l’attitude: mai ornamento, mai accessorio di stagione. Sempre il centro di gravità di ogni look. E il 2026 lo ha ricordato (ancora una volta) a tutti.
Guarda le nostre storie e leggi l’articolo completo di @cadeaudavril su L’OFFICIEL ITALIA

@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Nearly a year into his tenure as @Balenciaga’s creative director, Pierpaolo Piccioli finds himself stewarding one of fashion’s most consequential legacies. Founded by Cristóbal Balenciaga in 1917 and long regarded as a defining force within haute couture, the house enters a new era under the Italian designer’s direction—one guided as much by instinct as inheritance.⁠
⁠
After shaping the world of Valentino for more than two decades, @pppiccioli now prepares to write his own chapter within Balenciaga’s history. “I want to follow my instinct, to be able to go in any direction without betraying the past,” he tells L’OFFICIEL. “Balenciaga is creativity, and creativity is the map to follow.”⁠
⁠
For L’OFFICIEL, Piccioli curates a selection of Balenciaga images from the magazine’s archive and reflects on the weight—and freedom—of working within a house so deeply embedded in fashion history. ⁠
⁠
Photos courtesy of the L'OFFICIEL Archive⁠
⁠
#Balenciaga #PierpaoloPiccioli #CristobalBalenciaga #FashionHistory⁠

posts.caption:
Life imitates art, and Monday night’s Met Gala made that abundantly clear. With the dress code “Fashion is Art,” attendees pulled from everything spanning antiquity’s most iconic works to more niche, contemporary references. Designers, from @matthieu_blazy and Jonathan Anderson to @pppiccioli, fully embraced the theme through custom looks created for the night’s attendees, weaving in fine art references alongside archival codes from their respective houses, and turning “Fashion is Art” into wearable interpretation.
Here, we look to some of the art references of the night. @kendalljenner fully committed to the theme, channeling the ‘Winged Victory of Samothrace’ (190 BCE) in a sculptural look by @Gap Studio, designed by Zac Posen. @roses_are_rosie turned to the 20th century, drawing from Georges Braque’s ‘The Birds’—a nod to the cubist-turned-expressionist master—through a @YSL gown by @anthonyvaccarello, complete with Braque’s signature bird motif.
@madonna arrived with an entire entourage, reimagining Leonora Carrington’s ‘The Temptation of St. Anthony (Fragment II),’ while Lauren Sánchez wore @Schiaparelli in a look inspired by John Singer Sargent’s ‘Portrait of Madame X.’ With some interpretations more on the nose and others more nuanced, the overarching theme remained clear: fashion as a direct extension of art. For more on the fashion at the Met, visit the link in our bio!
Photos Getty Images

posts.caption:
For his first Cruise show at @Dior, Jonathan Anderson is heading to Los Angeles—and not quietly. He’ll be the first to stage a runway show at LACMA, turning the museum into Dior’s latest cinematic set.⁠
⁠
But this isn’t a random California detour. Dior’s connection to Hollywood runs deep. Actresses like #MarleneDietrich—who famously had Dior written into her contracts—blurred the line between character and couture, while #GraceKelly and #RitaHayworth turned those silhouettes into something bigger than fashion.⁠
⁠
And Hollywood did what Hollywood does best—it scaled the fantasy. Dior wasn’t just dressing women anymore; it was dressing the idea of a star. Film made those looks immortal, exporting them far beyond Paris and embedding them into pop culture.⁠
⁠
Photos Getty Images⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.

posts.caption:
Behind every “Made in Italy” luxury product is a network of artisans fighting to preserve generations of craftsmanship.
From Florence’s handmade shoemakers and silk ateliers to regional embroidery, tailoring, and leather workshops, Italian craftsmanship remains the backbone of global luxury manufacturing, producing nearly 40% of the world’s luxury goods. But despite its prestige, the industry faces growing pressure from disappearing artisanal skills, generational decline, and increasing competition from mass production.
Luxury houses including Prada, Dior, and Dolce & Gabbana have increasingly shifted focus toward protecting regional craftsmanship, spotlighting local artisans and investing in traditional techniques that define Italian luxury. The renewed demand for handmade products has also pushed “Made in Italy” beyond a label, turning it into a symbol of heritage, patience, and human artistry.
As fashion continues balancing scale with authenticity, Italy’s artisans are becoming more important than ever, not just as manufacturers, but as the cultural foundation behind modern luxury itself.

posts.caption:
Cristiano Ronaldo is expanding his empire beyond football with the launch of an ultra-exclusive private members club in Madrid.
Located in the city’s prestigious Golden Mile district, the upcoming “Vega Private Members Club” is designed around privacy, luxury, and elite networking, with one major rule standing out: mobile phones and photography will be completely banned inside the venue.
Spread across nearly 1,000 square metres, the space will function as a business and social hub, featuring private meeting rooms, coworking areas, podcast studios, fine dining spaces, curated bars, and a dedicated wine club. Memberships are expected to reach up to €15,000 for invitation-only founder access, alongside tailored experiences and lifetime privileges.
As Ronaldo continues building his post-football business portfolio across hospitality, fitness, fashion, and media, the Madrid club marks another step in transforming the CR7 brand into a global luxury lifestyle empire.
#Luxurious #CristianoRonaldo

posts.caption:
Jeff Bezos and Lauren Sánchez are pushing deeper into sustainable fashion through the Bezos Earth Fund, announcing $34 million in grants focused on developing next-generation eco-friendly fabrics.
The funding will support universities and research institutions working on biodegradable, plastic-free alternatives to materials like polyester, rayon, silk, and cotton — fabrics that currently contribute heavily to fashion’s environmental footprint.
Bezos Earth Fund CEO Tom Taylor described fossil fuel dependency as one of fashion’s biggest challenges, with the initiative aiming to make sustainable textiles more scalable, affordable, and commercially viable for the global industry.
The move signals a growing shift where billionaires, tech leaders, and climate-focused organizations are increasingly influencing the future of fashion through sustainability, material innovation, and long-term environmental investment.
#Luxurious #JeffBezos #Fashion

Nita Ambani brought Indian craftsmanship to the global stage once again as she attended the Venice Biennale for the return of the India Pavilion.
Wearing a custom Anamika Khanna chiffon-georgette saree in soft blush pink and powder blue tones, she paired the drape with a delicately embroidered lace blouse and a handwoven Butidar Sozni shawl, celebrating traditional Indian textile artistry through understated elegance.
The standout detail, however, was her sculptural Wallace Chan earrings, crafted from rosewood, titanium, sapphires, and rubies, designed as wearable works of art. The look reflected her continued effort to position Indian craftsmanship, heritage, and artisanal luxury at the centre of global cultural conversations.

#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.

Caleb Hearon, a comedian known for his podcast and his stand-up, joined the world of high fashion in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”
Alongside Meryl Streep, Stanley Tucci and Anne Hathaway, he plays an assistant to Miranda Priestly, the demanding, fictional editor of Runway magazine. Hearon’s character, nicknamed Charlie the Chair because he is not allowed to leave his desk, “is stepping into the shoes of Andy Sachs 20 years ago,” said David Frankel, who directed both films.
Hearon said it’s a role he never dreamed of booking. “It’s ‘The Devil Wears Prada’!" he said. "It is a fashion movie, and we still do not like fat people in this country. I was so pleasantly surprised at how far we’ve come.”
Hearon is circling the mainstream, and it’s obvious to those in his orbit. Regardless of his accomplishments, though, he still sees himself as the Caleb who was born and raised in rural Missouri. His Midwestern earnestness and affability draw people to him, but does his podcast-friendly personality translate into Hollywood stardom? posts.caption:
When “Fashion Is Art,” the dress code for the Met Gala on Monday night, was announced, it seemed like a risk. After all, fashion has been trying for decades to prove its bona fides as an equal of the other fine arts at the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
The gala has increasingly been walking the line between elegant and absurd, as guests attempt to one-up one another in the attention economy. Giving celebrities “art” as a theme could have inspired a host of old masters and Impressionism cosplay rather than any sort of consideration about what it actually means in fashion terms.
See Heidi Klum, who walked as a Grecian or Roman statue. Or Ben Platt in a Tanner Fletcher suit that evoked Georges Seurat’s “A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte.” Or Anne Hathaway, who had a Grecian figure appliquéd onto the skirt of her Michael Kors gown.
More than just flirt with costume, the Met Gala’s dress code also “acted as a reminder of the distinction between fashion and art,” wrote Vanessa Friedman, chief fashion critic for The New York Times. In her analysis, @vvfriedman examines guests’ interpretations of the dress code. Tap the link in our bio to read. Photo by @vnina

Long after the photographers had left the green cobblestone carpet of the Met Gala, celebrities filed into after-parties across New York City. At Boom, at the top of the Standard in the Meatpacking District of Manhattan, the designer Gabriela Hearst co-hosted a party with the actress Grace Gummer. Guests like ASAP Rocky and Lindsey Vonn mingled while Questlove spun vintage Chaka Khan.
Across town, the movie director Baz Luhrmann and his wife, the costume designer Catherine Martin, hosted a celebration at their East Village bar, Monsieur. At the Lower East Side nightclub the Box, scantily dressed dancers in sky-high heels shimmied beneath a disco ball before the D.J. Diplo arrived for his set.
The New York Times reporters @jacobbernsteinnyc and @smbharh14 caught up with the stars as they hit the after-parties across the city. Tap the link in our bio to get a look at who was out and what they wore. Photos by @rebeccasmeyne, @amirbangs and @vnina

posts.caption:
The Met Gala steps are a stage where celebrities strive not merely to look pretty, but to create indelible, avant-garde fashion moments. The most memorable looks tend to commit to the often abstract theme and take risks not typically seen on award show red carpets. Sometimes that works; other times it ends in disaster.
This year, guests stepped onto a mossy-looking, trompe l’oeil cobblestone carpet surrounded by a backdrop that recalled a Monet canvas. It was fitting for the evening’s brief: “Fashion is art.” It may be a bit on the nose for an event whose raison d’être is to raise money for the Metropolitan Museum of Art’s fashion-focused Costume Institute.
Of all of the looks from the Met Gala, presented in no particular order, these were our unequivocal favorites. Tap the link in our bio to see all 15 looks. Photos by @vnina and @amirbangs

posts.caption:
At this year’s Met Gala, some famous public figures were hidden behind masks, blindfolds, headpieces and makeup that created the illusion of a marble statue or an aging man. You might not know it to look at them, but the night was a celebration of the body.
Guests including Rihanna and ASAP Rocky, Doja Cat, Beyoncé and her daughter Blue Ivy showed out on Monday night, wearing outfits that riffed on the idea of “the dressed body,” the focus of the new fashion exhibition. That included corsets, foam, barely-there Saint Laurent gowns and different styles of flowers.
Dressed as a marble statue was Heidi Klum, wearing a look inspired by the Veiled Vestal, a 19th-century sculpture by Raffaelle Monti. She planned the look for three months and it took her five hours to get ready, she said, adding that it was the best her body had felt at a Met Gala. “For once, my feet don’t hurt,” she said, wiggling her marble-like toes in flat sandals.
The New York Times reporter @s_evangelina and the photographer @landonnordeman caught up with guests at the gala’s cocktail party. Tap the link in our bio to step inside the fete and read more about how guests spent their night at the museum.

posts.caption:
In the early 2010s, #Topshop was one of the hottest brands to wear to the #MetGala, dressing all the rising “It girls.” Over a decade later, fast fashion is borrowing more than ever from the credibility and visibility of luxury spaces like the Met, as a powerful marketing platform.
When a celebrity wears something like Zara by #JohnGalliano or Gap by #ZacPosen, it blurs the line between mass and luxury. Obviously, these dresses aren’t coming from the same places as the jeans and T-shirts we buy in stores, but they still function as cultural symbols and marketing tools.
Whether celebrities should wear these brands depends on what you think the Met Gala is meant to represent. On one hand, it reflects the current reality of fashion. On the other, it risks turning the event into a marketing vehicle for mass retailers and diluting the focus on craftsmanship and artistry that traditionally defined the gala.
What do you think?

posts.caption:
#PhilipTreacy’s ship hat is probably one of the most beautiful pieces of millinery I’ve ever seen, and #Madonna wearing it to the 2026 #MetGala was the perfect excuse to talk about it again.
Philip made the hat for Isabella Blow in 1994. He was inspired by the 18th-century Coiffure à la Belle-Poule, a nautical hairstyle that featured a model ship sailing atop a sea of thick, sculpted hair.
In 1995, #MichaelJackson reportedly offered $25,000 for the hat; however, Philip refused as he only had one. He said “even though I love Michael Jackson, I just didn’t want it going off to Neverland.” The piece went on to become an iconic moment in fashion history. It inspired designers like #JeanPaulGaultier, who revived the headpiece again for his 2025 couture collection in collaboration with Ludovic de Saint Sernin

posts.caption:
Last weekend, I double-featured The Devil Wears Prada with its freshly minted sequel. Two decades later, the sleek, cutthroat halls of Runway magazine are back, and Miranda Priestly is once again staring down the barrel of a shifting culture. Watching the two films back-to-back proved to be visceral in how culture stubbornly refuses to compromise.
Right now, if you spend any time observing Silicon Valley’s tech elite, there is a frantic, almost desperate obsession with one word: Taste.
The tech world has built the machines, they have trained the models, and now they are staring at the void, realising that algorithmic optimisation is not the same thing as art. They want culture and nuance.
In an era drowning in corporate-dictated digital slop and automated mediocrity, this sequel is a middle finger to the machine. It is fiercely, unapologetically anti-AI, and carries a necessary cultural correction. It prompts us to look around and ask who or what is choosing what we consume. Are we surrendering our aesthetics to the predictable, frictionless churn of artificial intelligence, or are we demanding the grit, the friction, and the divine arrogance of human curation?
Opinion by shalakakulkarni | Views expressed by the author are their own.

Coimbatore's Arunachalam Muruganantham, recognised as the inventor of a low-cost sanitary pad-making machine in India, has been nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize 2026. His machines, which can make sanitary pads for less than a third of the cost of commercial pads, have been installed in 23 of the 28 states of India in rural areas. He is reportedly planning to expand the production of these machines to 106 countries.
Muruganantham is credited with mobilising grassroots women by making hygienic sanitary products more accessible and raising awareness around menstruation. His story inspired the 2018 film Pad Man, starring Akshay Kumar and Radhika Apte.

And then came the little details that made everything feel almost dreamlike… pearl-draped entrances, pastel blooms catching the evening light, petals suspended mid-air as if even time wanted to stay a little longer. Every frame felt less like a grand celebration and more like two people slowly stepping into a forever they already trusted. 💫
Because sometimes, the most beautiful wedding stories aren’t the loudest ones.

posts.caption: If something needs such in-depth analysis to be understood than the assignment was a failure! It’s not a dissertation on the politics of 17th century France. It’s CLOTHING! It’s a fashion show and since fashion shows have been able to be seen by millions and consumed in photos and video for decades, those who are not in the loop to see the clothes for real are only left with what we see and to dare to say that just because a design has “References” or inner history negates it from being judged as what it simply LOOKS LIKE, is insultingly arrogant, something that takes 4,000 hours of the most intricate and elaborate technique… CAN STILL LOOK HORRID! All that time does not acquit it of its ugliness or stupidity of design! When all that history and back story sgd references WORK IN CONJUNCTION with the clothes to crest something magical (Blazy/Chanel) then the framework of all that in depth research and application of history is breathtaking, when it’s like JWA/Dior, it just comes across as over focus on ideas that then translate into nothing more than dissertations on a thought than the true purpose of what is happening… MAKING BEAUTIFUL CLOTHES! And for you to suggest that unless you’re erudite and informed of history that you shouldn’t comment is the height of condescension! Anyone can see! And no matter how layered the meaning, if it looks like all that time taken to produce garbage still looks like GARBAGE, it changes not its beauty because you have the knowing of the thought processes and HISTOIRE D’MAISON behind you! It’s pretentious and asinine

posts.caption:
Every stylist has been asked when they are going to get a real job.
The asking has not stopped because the answer has not landed yet. It will.
The invoices are not a side hustle. The portfolio is not a hobby. The training did not happen in a classroom but it happened. Years of it. On set. In showrooms. In hotel hallways. In customs offices. In the quiet hours after the shoot when nobody else is paying attention.
The industry knows it is a career. The contracts know. The agents know. The fact that the
people outside the industry are still catching up does not change what the work has always
A career is what you build when nobody hands you one. There is no clear path into styling. No certification. No straight line. That does not make it less of a career. It makes it more.
To every stylist who has had to defend the job at a family dinner, in a friend’s group chat, in a comment from someone who has never done it. The work speaks for itself eventually.

posts.caption:
There are things about being a fashion stylist that nobody outside the job will fully understand.
The hours spent looking for one specific thing. The cab ride home with eight bags. The returns that take longer than the shoot. The cry in the back of the taxi after a job that nobody knew was almost a disaster.
The pride of a pinned hem nobody will ever see. The PR email that reads friendly but is not. The way a stylist’s brain does not switch off, even at a dinner party.
This is the post for the stylists who recognise themselves in this. Save it. Send it to the friend
who will laugh. Send it to the assistant who is just starting and is wondering if it is supposed to
feel like this. It is.
The career looks glamorous and feels like work. Both are true. Anyone who says it is only one is wrong.
Comment STYLIST to be in a room with stylists who get it.
With love, Em x

posts.caption:
Fashion stylists are done explaining what they do.
Explaining what you actually do all day.
Explaining why the rate is what it is.
Explaining that prep is the work.
Explaining that a press credit is not a paycheque. Explaining that you are not available for this one, and no, you do not need to suggest someone else.
The explaining is exhausting. It is also the reason the industry stays the way it is. The more we explain, the more the next generation grows up thinking the work needs explaining at all.
This is the post for the stylists who are done. Done apologising for the rate. Done justifying the prep. Done turning visibility into compensation. Done shrinking the scope of the job to make it easier for someone else to understand.
The work is the work. The rate is the rate. The job is real and skilled and full and earned.
Comment STYLIST to find out how we can help. 💚
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on May 06, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'Things Fashion Stylists Are Done Explaining to People. STYLIST STYLISTELIXIR ELIXIR'.

posts.caption:
Working for credit is one of the biggest hidden costs in a styling career.
Every shoot taken for exposure is a shoot that is not being paid for. Every “great for your portfolio” is a polite way of asking for free labour. The industry runs on the assumption that stylists will keep saying yes because the next job might be the one that pays. Most of the time it isn’t.
Getting out of the credit cycle is not about turning more work down. It is about knowing how to spot the unpaid ask before saying yes, and how to push back without losing the relationship.
That is the conversation we have on the 1:1 calls. Where you are now. What is paying. What is not. And the script that gets you out of the credit cycle and into work that actually funds your career.
Book a 1:1 call: stylistelixir.com
With love, Em x​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

posts.caption: Give it some time. My suggestion, stay away from outright hostile people, approach space wary people with understanding ( I will explain), and curious people with warmth. posts.caption:
Every bar calendar now seems to have a guest shift on it.
What began as bartender swaps has become India’s new cocktail flex: visiting mixologists, limited-edition menus, global bar names and alcohol brands finding a smoother way into the craft conversation.
Awards gave bartenders star power. Brands found a marketing format that feels less like a hard sell. Drinkers got novelty without abandoning the comfort of a familiar bar.
The catch is obvious. When every night is programmed to feel special, special starts to feel scheduled. A bar can become known for hosting everyone else before it becomes known for itself.
Novelty brings people in. Comfort brings them back.

posts.caption:
At the Met Gala 2026, fashion has been framed as art, and celebrities have showcased clothing as material, labour, and object.
Karan Johar (@karanjohar) in Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05) treats the body as canvas, with painted and embroidered surfaces drawing from Raja Ravi Varma, while Malhotra’s own bandhgala foregrounds labour, displaying the process as a part of the final piece.
Siblings Gauravi Kumari (@gauravikumari) and Padmanabh Singh in Prabal Gurung (@pachojaipur) have built directly from archive and atelier. Gauravi’s look incorporates her grandmother, Maharani Gayatri Devi’s chiffon sari into a couture structure that preserves its drape, while Padmanabh’s Phulghar coat, developed over 600+ hours in velvet with aari, zardozi, dabka and resham work, turns Rajasthani craft into surface and object.
Natasha Poonawalla (@natasha.poonawalla) in Dolce & Gabbana × Marc Quinn shifts fashion into sculpture, centring an orchid form as the primary object, with the body acting as its base.
By drawing on craft traditions, art-historical references, and material-led construction, these celebrities have positioned their presence as part of a larger exchange where fashion operates as a legitimate artistic language on an international stage. They’re not merely wearers of couture.
#met2026

posts.caption:
I know everyone is still locked into Met Gala discourse, but we can’t forget to wish a happy belated birthday to the late Audrey Hepburn.
Hepburn completely shifted the fashion landscape of the 1950s and 60s away from excess and toward something cleaner, sharper, and infinitely more timeless. Through her partnership with Hubert de Givenchy, she helped define a new kind of elegance built on simplicity, tailoring, and restraint—most famously in Breakfast at Tiffany’s and that iconic black dress.
What made her style endure wasn’t trend-chasing. It was consistency. She understood personal style before the internet turned it into content.
She’ll always be our girl.
#AudreyHepburn #Givenchy #FashionHistory #BreakfastAtTiffanys #FashionFilm

posts.caption:
Initial read on the 2026 Met Gala is giving a disconnect between concept and execution. A theme as expansive as “Costume Art” requires clarity of interpretation—and so far, that clarity feels uneven.
We got off to a bit of a rough start. Let’s be real: attendees aren’t really dressing on theme. Yes, there are referential looks (and those matter), but referencing something isn’t the same as executing it well. Right now, it’s reading more underwhelming than intentional.
That said, there are highlights. Robert Wun is consistently delivering, and Beyoncé is officially back after a decade, attending with Blue Ivy Carter, both in custom Olivier Rousteing. The moment we’ve all been waiting for.
Some strong moments—just not enough of them yet. More coverage soon.
#MetGala #CostumeArt #FashionIsArt #TheCitizensPoste #Beyoncé

posts.caption:
The Met Gala is officially live in NYC—and the 2026 theme, “Costume Art,” is… being interpreted. But we do have some favorites so far. With the focus on the body as canvas, some looks are leaning more sculptural than others. Robert Wun (notably on Lisa and Naomi Osaka) is delivering strong structure and veiling, while Schiaparelli under Daniel Roseberry is doing its usual anatomical surrealism.
Burç Aykol is also exploring the body more directly through drapery, and Heidi Klum (as always) committed to a full sculpture moment.
Overall: a few clear interpretations, a lot of safe ones. Who’s standing out to you?
Images: Getty
#MetGala #CostumeArt #FashionIsArt #RobertWun #Schiaparelli

posts.caption:
Happy Met Gala Monday to those who celebrate—and if you don’t, consider this your invitation. Tonight is one of the few moments where fashion fully takes center stage. The Met Gala isn’t just a red carpet; it’s a funding mechanism for the Costume Institute, a form of preservation, and one of the only spaces where fashion is treated with the same cultural weight as art.
And with that comes nuance. You can love the spectacle—the looks, the drama, the commentary—while also questioning the structure, the exclusivity, and what it represents. Both can exist at once.
Enjoy it. Analyze it. Have opinions. And as always, stay tuned for all of The Citizen’s Poste’s coverage tonight.
#MetGala #FashionCulture #RedCarpet #MetGala2026 #CostumeInstitute

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Growing up in Chennai as the youngest of three siblings, acting wasn’t part of the plan for model student Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). During her gap year after college, the then 21-year-old dabbled in modelling and ended up at a film audition for kicks. “I thought if I do get it, I’ll do it for a year and then go back to studying,” she says. “The idea was to do it temporarily, never make it a career. I’m sure my teachers were disappointed when I became an actor. I think some of them still are,” she laughs.
We’re not the first to ask her what her alternative career would be, but this time she has an answer: “I’m doing it now,” she says with excitement. “I think this entrepreneurial journey is what I’ve always wanted to do,” says the serial entrepreneur, whose work straddles films, beauty, wellness, fashion, food, and sport, all at once.
For our second anniversary issue, @chloechou13 flew to Hyderabad to chat with our cover star. Full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Samantha Ruth Prabhu’s (@samantharuthprabhuoffl) face card is immaculate. We notice this on a Friday morning, when we are on the set of our May cover shoot in Mumbai, and the star is getting ready for her first shot. At some point, the topic moves on to her upcoming film, and someone in the room asks, “Who’s the hero?” Samantha simply says, “I don’t do those kinds of films anymore.” We can’t help but grin.
It’s been a busy seven days for the star, between promotions and shooting patchwork for her upcoming film ‘Maa Inti Bangaaram’. “The story will be the hero in the movies that I do. The story is bigger than the producer, bigger than the actors in it.”
It isn’t about occupying the lead role on the top of the call sheet, she explains. In her career, Samantha has, in several of her biggest films, played characters orbiting male superstars, and knows all too well that female characters are often relegated to the role of a catalyst in the male protagonist’s journey, to propel his emotional arc as a narrative device. She, however, counterbalanced them with more textured roles, such as the journalist and storyteller Madhuravani in ‘Mahanati’ (2018), the emotionally complex Vaembu in the cult neo-noir ‘Super Deluxe’ (2019), and the 70-year-old trapped in a younger body as Swathi in ‘Oh! Baby’ (2019).

posts.caption:
For our second anniversary issue, there was no looking beyond Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). Two years ago, she almost quit acting when she was diagnosed with myositis, an autoimmune condition that causes muscle weakness, fatigue, and pain. It forced her to slow down and taught her a lesson at peak fame that she will never forget. “I was just so focused on running this race for which I don’t even know what the prize at the finish line was,” recalls the actor of the time when everything came to a grinding halt.
Since then, she’s renegotiated everything: her work ethic, priorities, even brand deals. “I realised all the checklists that I was so eager to tick off weren’t my own. They were checklists made for me by other people,” she says. Now at 39, she’s not only a top-billed actor but also an audacious producer, a serial entrepreneur, a fierce investor, and a chatty podcast host. At the link in bio, the multi-hyphenate talks to @chloechou13 about success, silent retreats, swimming, and what is likely to be her joyful next chapter.

Let’s be honest—for the longest time, playing sports meant settling for a dusty, barren plot of land next door or a dimly lit turf tucked behind a warehouse. Well, there’s finally a platform that treats your game time with the same aesthetic and social respect as your dinner plans: @district.play.
District already built itself around the idea of going out better—concerts, dining plans, movies, experiences, the whole social calendar. Now with District Play, it’s stepping into the sports era too, turning cricket, football, badminton, pickleball and more into something far more seamless, spontaneous, and actually doable.
And modern India? It clearly wants to play a little more seriously. After all, it’s become the modern-day social plan. The new catch-up. The new after-work decompress ritual. People are dressing for it, building communities around it, romanticising it, and increasingly choosing it over another passive night out.
So, take this as a sign to go from a meeting to a match (and back again) without missing a beat, and having fun while at it.

posts.caption:
MET Gala: Beauty’s Starring Role
This year the MET Gala explored fashion as art and beauty took up a starring role. The most experimental and our favourite from the night? Anok Yai’s face, painted in gold with 3D tear drops glistening down referencing the iconic Black Madonna sculpture. Another favourite included Bad Bunny’s take on the “ageing body”. In the age of baby botox, collagen shots and facelifts, he walked up the MET stairs with a cane in tow and an aged-prosthetic face, “53 years” in the making.
Reliance Retail Acquires Priyanka Chopra’s Anomaly
Lifestyle conglomerate Reliance Retail has acquired actor Priyanka Chopra Jonas’s global haircare brand, Anomaly. First launched in 2021, Anomaly has established itself in the global market for its clean and vegan formulations.
Footnote: Birkenstock’s Nail Paint Venture
The 250-year old German footwear company, Birkenstock, which is popular for its orthopedic-focused sandals with a specialised footbed design has launched a glossy range of five vegan, plant-based nail paints crafted to match the colour palette of its Arizona slippers. These polishes, priced at $12 each, are sold alongside a clear base and a top coat.
Maison Margiela’s Couture Fragrances
Drawing inspiration from the world of haute couture, the L’Oréal Group-owned Maison Margiela has recently unveiled a new lineup of artisanal haute fragrances. Titled Scentsorium, the six scents were three years in the making by the luxury maison’s former creative head John Galliano and have been released as an aromatic equivalent of its couture clothing.
SK-II Debuts in India
The luxe legacy beauty label SK-II has finally debuted in the Indian market courtesy LUXASIA, the omnichannel beauty distributor.
Attended by Hollywood actor and global brand ambassador Anna Sawai, the immersive launch event last month further noted a Magic Scan—the AI driven analysis for attendees, exploring skin-age and other concerns.
